source_text:
It was a beautiful summer evening and I decided
to go for a swim off Doolin Pier in County Clare,
Ireland, where I moved in 2012. There was a
woman in the water with Dusty, a dolphin who
has a great relationship with a group of people
she regularly swims with.

Dusty arrived in Doolin in about 2008 and
hundreds of people have swum with her since,
giving the impression that she’s totally tame.
She has even starred in an Irish tourist-board ad
campaign in which a girl says she would like to
touch a dolphin.

That evening, this woman was tickling Dusty’s
tummy and it just looked so inviting. There were
about 20 tourists and locals on the pier, looking
at this lovely spectacle. Just after I got into the
water, Dusty left the woman she was with and
went ballistic – I found out afterwards that she’s
very territorial when she is with somebody. Her
tail was flapping wildly and, at first, I thought it
was a display but, then, I twigged: maybe she’s
angry. I knew I had to get out of the water so I
swam towards the pier but, within microseconds,
Dusty had ploughed into me with her snout. It
was very powerful and painful, and the speed
was amazing. I went hurtling forwards.

All these people on the pier were staring down
at me open-mouthed. Dusty was still in the water
beside me, her tail flapping crazily. That was
more frightening than anything: I thought, if she
hits me with her tail, I could go under; I’m gone.

I was at the pier but couldn’t get out because of
my injuries. I felt pure terror. I shouted for help
and a guy put his arm in and pulled me out on to
the steps. Then, another man appeared and said
he was an orthopaedic surgeon who specialized
in marine trauma. He had been driving into Doolin
when he saw what was happening on the pier.
I was so cold and very worried – I didn’t know
how bad my injuries were and my biggest fear
was internal bleeding. He checked me over and
was very reassuring, saying he couldn’t feel any
evidence of it but that I probably had broken
bones. I found out later that I had six spinal
fractures, three broken ribs and a damaged lung.

I was in hospital for five days, in a back brace
for several weeks and off work for five months
with limited mobility, stiffness and pain. Then, I
was diagnosed with post-traumatic stress. My
near-death experience had left me anxious about
everything and overreacting in a way I had never
done before. I felt that people were looking at
me in the wrong way, I began to struggle with
loud noises and I suffered from memory loss.
Three months before the accident, I had opened
a health-food shop but I had to let it go because
I could no longer work. It was the toughest
year ever but, now, it’s all behind me. I had
craniosacral therapy, osteopathy and massage,
and am building up my own osteopathy practice
now. I have a new empathy with patients because
I have been one.

I am grateful that I am healthy and I really want to
prevent other people being injured. We have this
lovely idea about dolphins and have faith in them
– who would think a dolphin would ever attack a

That evening, the woman was tickling Dusty’s tummy. It looked friendly. About 20 tourists and local people were on the pier watching.

When I got into the water, Dusty left the woman and suddenly became very wild. Later I learned that she is very territorial when she is with someone. Her tail was moving fast. At first, I thought she was showing off, but then I thought she might be angry.

I tried to swim to the pier to get out. But very quickly, Dusty hit me hard with her nose. It was very strong and very painful. I was pushed forward.

People on the pier stared at me. Dusty stayed next to me and her tail was still moving wildly. That scared me most. I thought if she hit me with her tail, I could go under the water and die.

I reached the pier, but I could not get out because I was hurt. I felt very afraid. I shouted for help. A man put his arm in the water and pulled me onto the steps.

Then another man came. He said he was an orthopaedic surgeon and he specialized in marine trauma. He had been driving into Doolin and saw what was happening. I was very cold and very worried. My biggest fear was bleeding inside my body. He checked me and said he could not feel signs of internal bleeding, but he thought I might have broken bones.

Later, I learned my injuries were serious. I had six fractures in my spine, three broken ribs, and a damaged lung.

I stayed in hospital for five days. I wore a back brace for several weeks. I could not work for five months. I had limited movement, stiffness, and pain.

Then doctors said I had post-traumatic stress. After the near-death experience, I felt anxious about everything and overreacted. I felt people were looking at me the wrong way. Loud noises were hard for me. I also had memory loss.

Three months before the accident, I opened a health-food shop. But I had to give it up because I could not work. It was the hardest year, but now it is behind me.

I had craniosacral therapy, osteopathy, and massage. Now I am building my own osteopathy practice. I understand patients better because I have been a patient.

I am thankful that I am healthy. I want to stop other people from getting hurt. People have a nice idea about dolphins and trust them. Who would think a dolphin could attack a person? If you see a very dangerous animal coming with its teeth showing, you feel scared. But dolphins have a wide smile.

I am not angry with Dusty. I respect her. But I was in her territory, and she is a wild and unpredictable animal. People need to know this. Many people come to swim with her, and they do not understand how dangerous it can be. People said my injuries were among several injuries that summer.

After I was pulled out, Dusty swam away. Then she came back. She was upright in the water next to me and looked at me. We looked into each other’s eyes. I felt she was truly sorry. She seemed like a different dolphin, and the anger was gone. People on the pier were amazed. After that moment, my terror ended. I made peace with her.

woman in the water with Dusty, a dolphin who
has a great relationship with a group of people
she regularly swims with.

Dusty arrived in Doolin in about 2008 and
hundreds of people have swum with her since,
giving the impression that she’s totally tame.
She has even starred in an Irish tourist-board ad
campaign in which a girl says she would like to
touch a dolphin.

That evening, this woman was tickling Dusty’s
tummy and it just looked so inviting. There were
about 20 tourists and locals on the pier, looking
at this lovely spectacle. Just after I got into the
water, Dusty left the woman she was with and
went ballistic – I found out afterwards that she’s
very territorial when she is with somebody. Her
tail was flapping wildly and, at first, I thought it
was a display but, then, I twigged: maybe she’s
angry. I knew I had to get out of the water so I
swam towards the pier but, within microseconds,
Dusty had ploughed into me with her snout. It
was very powerful and painful, and the speed
was amazing. I went hurtling forwards.

All these people on the pier were staring down
at me open-mouthed. Dusty was still in the water
beside me, her tail flapping crazily. That was
more frightening than anything: I thought, if she
hits me with her tail, I could go under; I’m gone.

I was at the pier but couldn’t get out because of
my injuries. I felt pure terror. I shouted for help
and a guy put his arm in and pulled me out on to
the steps. Then, another man appeared and said
he was an orthopaedic surgeon who specialized
in marine trauma. He had been driving into Doolin
when he saw what was happening on the pier.
I was so cold and very worried – I didn’t know
how bad my injuries were and my biggest fear
was internal bleeding. He checked me over and
was very reassuring, saying he couldn’t feel any
evidence of it but that I probably had broken
bones. I found out later that I had six spinal
fractures, three broken ribs and a damaged lung.

I was in hospital for five days, in a back brace
for several weeks and off work for five months
with limited mobility, stiffness and pain. Then, I
was diagnosed with post-traumatic stress. My
near-death experience had left me anxious about
everything and overreacting in a way I had never
done before. I felt that people were looking at
me in the wrong way, I began to struggle with
loud noises and I suffered from memory loss.
Three months before the accident, I had opened
a health-food shop but I had to let it go because
I could no longer work. It was the toughest
year ever but, now, it’s all behind me. I had
craniosacral therapy, osteopathy and massage,
and am building up my own osteopathy practice
now. I have a new empathy with patients because
I have been one.

I am grateful that I am healthy and I really want to
prevent other people being injured. We have this
lovely idea about dolphins and have faith in them
– who would think a dolphin would ever attack a
person? If you see a ferocious animal coming at
you with its teeth bared, it’s scary, but dolphins
have this lovely, wide smile.

I don’t have any anger towards Dusty. I respect
her. But I was in her territory and she’s a wild,
unpredictable animal. People need to know that.
So many come here to swim with her and they
don’t understand how dangerous it can be. Mine
were reportedly among several injuries
that summer.

After the man pulled me out of the water, Dusty
swam away but, then, she came back and was
bobbing vertically next to me, looking at me.
We locked eyes and I felt there was complete
remorse in her. She was a totally different
dolphin; the anger had gone. The people on
the pier were in awe. When she had that little
moment with me, that was the end of the terror. I
made my peace with her.

Then another man came and said he was an orthopaedic surgeon who worked with injuries from the sea. He had been driving into Doolin when he saw what was happening.

I was very cold and very worried. I did not know how bad my injuries were. My biggest fear was bleeding inside my body. He examined me and tried to calm me. He said he could not feel signs of internal bleeding, but he thought I might have broken bones. Later I learned I had six fractures in my spine, three broken ribs, and a damaged lung.

I stayed in the hospital for five days. I wore a back brace for several weeks. I could not work for five months, and I had trouble moving, with stiffness and pain. Then doctors said I had post-traumatic stress. The near-death experience made me anxious about everything. I reacted in ways I never had before. I felt like people were looking at me badly. Loud noises became hard to deal with, and I had memory loss.

Three months before the accident, I had opened a health-food shop. But I had to give it up because I could not work anymore. That year was the hardest of my life, but now it is behind me. I had craniosacral therapy, osteopathy, and massage. Now I am building my own osteopathy practice. I understand patients better because I have been one.

I am thankful that I am healthy. I really want to stop other people from getting hurt. People often think dolphins are always gentle and safe. Who would think a dolphin would attack a person? If a dangerous animal comes at you with its teeth showing, you feel afraid right away. But dolphins have a wide smile, so they do not look scary.

I am not angry at Dusty. I respect her. But I was in her area, and she is a wild animal that can act in unexpected ways. People need to know this. Many people come to swim with her, and they do not understand how dangerous it can be. I was told my injuries were one of several injuries that summer.

After the man pulled me out, Dusty swam away. Then she came back and floated upright next to me, looking at me. We looked into each other’s eyes. I felt she was sorry. She seemed like a different dolphin, and the anger was gone. The people on the pier were amazed. After that moment, I was no longer terrified. I felt at peace with her.

simplified_text:
Some cities are trying unusual ideas to make life better. City life can be fun, but it is not always good for the environment. People in cities often face pollution, traffic, and fewer green spaces. Here are five creative ideas.

Pop-up parks
Many cities seem built more for cars than for people. A pop-up park is a simple idea: turn an empty parking space into a small park with a few plants. The PARK(ing) project started as an art experiment in San Francisco and later spread around the world. People have also made temporary city farms and ecology demonstrations. The movement has a day of action every September.

Underground bike storage
Not everyone in a city uses a car. More people are riding bikes, but they need a safe place to keep them. A Tokyo engineering company called Giken designed an “eco-cycle, anti-seismic underground bicycle park.” It is a round storage space that is only seven metres wide and goes deep underground. It can hold 204 bikes. Owners press a button, and the system brings their bike up in about 13 seconds.

Glow-in-the-dark trees
Most people think of holiday lights when they imagine glowing trees, but Dutch designer-artist Daan Roosegaarde has another idea. He created an experimental “bioluminescent” plant. The technology mixes DNA from glowing sea bacteria with part of a plant’s DNA to make a glow like a jellyfish. Trials are happening to make a larger version. Roosegaarde hopes it could one day replace normal street lights.

Footstep power
In West Ham in east London, many commuters and shoppers walk over a raised walkway near the underground station. Some may feel the surface move slightly under their feet. This small movement helps power the streetlights above. The floor has smart tiles that collect energy from footsteps and turn it into electricity. The UK company Pavegen made this system and also installed it at London’s Heathrow Airport and other places.

Supertrees
Singapore’s Gardens by the Bay has man-made “supertrees.” They are steel-framed and can be up to 50 metres high. Flowers and ferns grow on them. Their canopies help take in and spread out heat. They can also collect rainwater and help ventilate two climate-controlled biomes (large conservatories) below. Eleven of the 18 trees also have solar panels on their branches.

source_text:
James Hamblin, senior editor of American magazine The Atlantic, recently joined the unwashed masses. As part of his series, ÔIf Our Bodies Could TalkÕ, Hamblin, a relatively sane- looking man, took on a no-showering challenge to examine the effect of over-cleansing the body. He reduced the number of showers he had and eliminated shampoo and soap when he did.

In doing so, he discovered what thousands of others have: the more we fervently try to clean ourselves with soaps, body washes and those silly little body polishers, the harder our skin works to restore equilibrium, encouraging usto begin the whole bewildering process again. Showering strips the skin of its own oil and bacteria Ð which, many would argue, is thewhole point of showering Ð but, apparently, this sometimes works a little too well, especially when you add hot water and cleansing products tothe mix.

You know that feeling after a shower when you feel like youÕre stuck in a skin suit two sizestoo small? ThatÕs because much of your skinÕs natural moisture has been washed down the drain. Additionally, our skin, much like our gut, plays host to millions of bene cial bacteria. Showering destroys these happy bacterial colonies; theyÕre completely wiped out by all of our frequent rubbing and scrubbing. And, when the bacteria washed off by soap repopulate, they tend to favour microbes which produce an odour Ð yes, too-frequent showering may actually make you smell more. When you stop showering and using soap, however, your skin goes through an initial (likely gross) adjustment period, after which the skin typically restores balance, oil production slows and healthy bacteria  ourish.

After everything was said and done, Hamblin realized what other no-soap/no-shower devotees have known for years: that the human body, functioning on its own, is actually quite lovely.ItÕs not just scent or aesthetics, either Ð although dermatologists suggest that slowing downon soap use can improve acne and eczema. Reducing the frequency of showers (and the number of cleansing products used) has very real implications for our environment. The average shower lasts seven minutes and uses 65 litres of water. ThatÕs 65 litres of clean, drinkable water that weÕre infusing with soap and washing down the drain each and every day Ð sometimes more than once.

The vital importance of clean water is becoming harder and harder to ignore, as California enters another summer of drought. ItÕs becoming clear that clean water is one of the worldÕs most valuable commodities and one that will soon be in short supply. Add in the environmental effect of all those body wash bottles and youÕve got yourself a handful of very compelling reasons to let your body go au naturel.

If this whole thing is giving you the heebie-jeebies as you recall the last time you were cheek byjowl with those who evidently already skimp on the showers Ð and have clearly not reached the scent-free stage yet Ð relax. Many cleansing- reduction enthusiasts still use deodorant if they  nd it necessary (everyoneÕs natural scent varies in intensity and can be affected by a host of factors including diet, hydration and exercise) and hand-washing with soap is still recommended as a vital way to reduce the spread ofinfectious diseases.

You donÕt need to go cold turkey, as Hamblin did, but in between thrice-daily disinfecting sessions and giving up showers altogether there lies room for our familiar, simple advice. Reduce. Skip a few showers, put down the soap and let those lovely little bacteria  ourish a little, would you?
simplified_text:
James Hamblin is a senior editor at The Atlantic. He did a challenge where he did not shower much. This was for his series, “If Our Bodies Could Talk.” He wanted to see what happens when people clean their bodies too much. He took fewer showers and stopped using shampoo and soap.

He learned something many others have learned. When we use lots of soap and body wash, our skin tries harder to balance itself again. Showering can wash away the skin’s natural oil and bacteria. Hot water and cleaning products can wash away too much.

After a shower, your skin may feel very tight. This is because a lot of your skin’s moisture is gone. Skin also has millions of helpful bacteria, like the gut does. Frequent scrubbing and soap can kill these bacteria. When new bacteria grow back, they may cause more smell. So showering too often can sometimes make you smell worse. When you stop showering and using soap, there can be an early period that feels unpleasant. After that, the skin often balances again, makes less oil, and healthy bacteria grow.

After his challenge, Hamblin felt the human body can be nice on its own. Dermatologists also say using less soap can help acne and eczema. Showering less can also help the environment. The average shower lasts seven minutes and uses 65 litres of water. That is 65 litres of clean, drinkable water mixed with soap and sent down the drain every day, and sometimes more than once a day.

Clean water is very important, and it is becoming harder to ignore this, as California has another summer of drought. Clean water is one of the world’s most valuable resources, and it may soon be in short supply. There is also waste from body wash bottles. These are reasons to let your body be more natural.

If this idea makes you feel nervous, relax. Many people who shower less still use deodorant when needed. Everyone’s natural smell is different, and it can change with diet, hydration, and exercise. Also, washing hands with soap is still recommended to help stop infectious diseases from spreading.

You do not have to stop showering all at once like Hamblin. But you can try a simple idea: reduce. Skip a few showers, use less soap, and let the helpful bacteria grow a little.

Showering strips the skin of its own oil and bacteria Ð which, many would argue, is thewhole point of showering Ð but, apparently, this sometimes works a little too well, especially when you add hot water and cleansing products tothe mix.

You know that feeling after a shower when you feel like youÕre stuck in a skin suit two sizestoo small? ThatÕs because much of your skinÕs natural moisture has been washed down the drain. Additionally, our skin, much like our gut, plays host to millions of bene cial bacteria. Showering destroys these happy bacterial colonies; theyÕre completely wiped out by all of our frequent rubbing and scrubbing. And, when the bacteria washed off by soap repopulate, they tend to favour microbes which produce an odour Ð yes, too-frequent showering may actually make you smell more. When you stop showering and using soap, however, your skin goes through an initial (likely gross) adjustment period, after which the skin typically restores balance, oil production slows and healthy bacteria  ourish.

After everything was said and done, Hamblin realized what other no-soap/no-shower devotees have known for years: that the human body, functioning on its own, is actually quite lovely.ItÕs not just scent or aesthetics, either Ð although dermatologists suggest that slowing downon soap use can improve acne and eczema. Reducing the frequency of showers (and the number of cleansing products used) has very real implications for our environment. The average shower lasts seven minutes and uses 65 litres of water. ThatÕs 65 litres of clean, drinkable water that weÕre infusing with soap and washing down the drain each and every day Ð sometimes more than once.

The vital importance of clean water is becoming harder and harder to ignore, as California enters another summer of drought. ItÕs becoming clear that clean water is one of the worldÕs most valuable commodities and one that will soon be in short supply. Add in the environmental effect of all those body wash bottles and youÕve got yourself a handful of very compelling reasons to let your body go au naturel.

If this whole thing is giving you the heebie-jeebies as you recall the last time you were cheek byjowl with those who evidently already skimp on the showers Ð and have clearly not reached the scent-free stage yet Ð relax. Many cleansing- reduction enthusiasts still use deodorant if they  nd it necessary (everyoneÕs natural scent varies in intensity and can be affected by a host of factors including diet, hydration and exercise) and hand-washing with soap is still recommended as a vital way to reduce the spread ofinfectious diseases.

You donÕt need to go cold turkey, as Hamblin did, but in between thrice-daily disinfecting sessions and giving up showers altogether there lies room for our familiar, simple advice. Reduce. Skip a few showers, put down the soap and let those lovely little bacteria  ourish a little, would you?

Frequent washing and scrubbing can kill them. When the bacteria grow back, the new microbes may cause more smell. So, showering too often might make you smell more. Everyone’s natural smell is different, and it can change with diet, hydration, and exercise. Also, washing your hands with soap is still important to help stop infectious diseases from spreading.

He reduced the number of showers he had and eliminated shampoo and soap when he did.

In doing so, he discovered what thousands of others have: the more we fervently try to clean ourselves with soaps, body washes and those silly little body polishers, the harder our skin works to restore equilibrium, encouraging usto begin the whole bewildering process again. Showering strips the skin of its own oil and bacteria Ð which, many would argue, is thewhole point of showering Ð but, apparently, this sometimes works a little too well, especially when you add hot water and cleansing products tothe mix.

When you stop showering and using soap, however, your skin goes through an initial (likely gross) adjustment period, after which the skin typically restores balance, oil production slows and healthy bacteria  ourish.

ThatÕs 65 litres of clean, drinkable water that weÕre infusing with soap and washing down the drain each and every day Ð sometimes more than once.

ItÕs becoming clear that clean water is one of the worldÕs most valuable commodities and one that will soon be in short supply. story_id: WNL are you a bad boyfriend
source_text:
In typical bad-boyfriend style, Dan Sullivan was
late to breakfast with the Guardian because he
got pulled over on his motorcycle.

Sullivan works too much, he says. He misses
dinner dates. He forgets to give presents.

And so, like seemingly everyone in Silicon Valley,
the 27-year-old venture capitalist has come up
with a start-up to fix it: BetterBoyfriend.me, a
service that sends girlfriends and wives a present
every month for about $70.

Sullivan, who works in finance, has been quietly
testing the program and has signed up about 350
boyfriends generating about $17,000 revenue
every month. Most members, he says, are from
his collection of Harvard and investor friends:
venture capitalists, founders and employees of
companies like Apple, Google and Facebook,
whose girlfriends have been getting presents
from Sullivan for the last year.

Each month, Sullivan’s members choose from a
list of seven possible gifts (chocolate, tea sets,
manicures). The package is sent to the boyfriend
unbranded and unlabelled.

He tests his ideas by buying Facebook ads
just to see what kind of traction they get.
BetterBoyfriend.me took off. “BetterBoyfriend
hit something,” Sullivan said. “It’s a pain point
everyone has.”

BetterBoyfriend.me was not Sullivan’s first idea.
For a couple of days, he thought about a
dog-sharing pitch: “I see all these rich guys
looking just miserable walking dogs and I
realized: a dog is only valuable 5% of the time,”
he said. “So, what if we got a guy who would
babysit the dogs and other people could rent
them out?”

Another start-up, called InvisibleBoyfriend, allows
users “to design and build a believable social
partner who is everything you want and is always
available to talk, whenever you want”. The end
result is a “boyfriend” who can text with you
all day.

Founder Kyle Tabor launched it to give users
a faux boyfriend to deter unwanted advances
but has found people have grown attached to
their algorithmic partners: “Many more users are
seeking companionship through conversation
rather than external ‘proof’ of a relationship to get
people off their back.”

For Sullivan and Tabor, the surprise was the
real relationships that have formed with their
customers. Sullivan says he has begun to see
himself as a sort of relationship consultant for
the boyfriends.

Sullivan admits he’s made mistakes. Early on, the
packages included receipts that had his name on
them, Dan Sullivan. “One of the boyfriends wrote
to me and said, ‘Listen, dude, she’s not mad but
Cynthia found out’."

About 50% of the women BetterBoyfriend.me
deliver packages to know about his involvement
in their relationships: “It’s correlated with age. I
think, after you’ve been married a while, you don’t
keep many secrets.”

And over the year, the young founder says he’s
got to know the boyfriends really well. They’ve
even done a delivery to a hospital delivery
room. The key, he said, is to remember that his
relationship is with the boyfriend.

When he first launched his company, he attached
tags that said BetterBoyfriend.me to flower
bouquets and set off for Dolores Park, a popular
lounging spot in the tech- and youth-heavy
Mission District.

“I looked for couples and gave the bouquet to
the girl but that didn’t go down well with the
boyfriend. Like, not at all. It made sense, though.
It was cool,” he said. “So, I reconsidered and
started handing them out to the boyfriends.”

He looked for couples and gave the bouquet to the girl. This did not go well with the boyfriend. So he changed his plan and started giving the bouquets to the boyfriends.

late to breakfast with the Guardian because he
got pulled over on his motorcycle.

Sullivan works too much, he says. He misses
dinner dates. He forgets to give presents.

And so, like seemingly everyone in Silicon Valley,
the 27-year-old venture capitalist has come up
with a start-up to fix it: BetterBoyfriend.me, a
service that sends girlfriends and wives a present
every month for about $70.

Sullivan, who works in finance, has been quietly
testing the program and has signed up about 350
boyfriends generating about $17,000 revenue
every month. Most members, he says, are from
his collection of Harvard and investor friends:
venture capitalists, founders and employees of
companies like Apple, Google and Facebook,
whose girlfriends have been getting presents
from Sullivan for the last year.

Each month, Sullivan’s members choose from a
list of seven possible gifts (chocolate, tea sets,
manicures). The package is sent to the boyfriend
unbranded and unlabelled.

He tests his ideas by buying Facebook ads
just to see what kind of traction they get.
BetterBoyfriend.me took off. “BetterBoyfriend
hit something,” Sullivan said. “It’s a pain point
everyone has.”

BetterBoyfriend.me was not Sullivan’s first idea.
For a couple of days, he thought about a
dog-sharing pitch: “I see all these rich guys
looking just miserable walking dogs and I
realized: a dog is only valuable 5% of the time,”
he said. “So, what if we got a guy who would
babysit the dogs and other people could rent
them out?”

Another start-up, called InvisibleBoyfriend, allows
users “to design and build a believable social
partner who is everything you want and is always
available to talk, whenever you want”. The end
result is a “boyfriend” who can text with you
all day.

Founder Kyle Tabor launched it to give users
a faux boyfriend to deter unwanted advances
but has found people have grown attached to
their algorithmic partners: “Many more users are
seeking companionship through conversation
rather than external ‘proof’ of a relationship to get
people off their back.”

For Sullivan and Tabor, the surprise was the
real relationships that have formed with their
customers. Sullivan says he has begun to see
himself as a sort of relationship consultant for
the boyfriends.

Sullivan admits he’s made mistakes. Early on, the
packages included receipts that had his name on
them, Dan Sullivan. “One of the boyfriends wrote
to me and said, ‘Listen, dude, she’s not mad but
Cynthia found out’."

About 50% of the women BetterBoyfriend.me
deliver packages to know about his involvement
in their relationships: “It’s correlated with age. I
think, after you’ve been married a while, you don’t
keep many secrets.”

And over the year, the young founder says he’s
got to know the boyfriends really well. They’ve
even done a delivery to a hospital delivery
room. The key, he said, is to remember that his
relationship is with the boyfriend.

When he first launched his company, he attached
tags that said BetterBoyfriend.me to flower
bouquets and set off for Dolores Park, a popular
lounging spot in the tech- and youth-heavy
Mission District.

“I looked for couples and gave the bouquet to
the girl but that didn’t go down well with the
boyfriend. Like, not at all. It made sense, though.
It was cool,” he said. “So, I reconsidered and
started handing them out to the boyfriends.”
simplified_text:
Dan Sullivan was late to breakfast with the Guardian. He was stopped by the police while riding his motorcycle.

Sullivan says he works too much. He misses dinner dates. He forgets to give gifts.

So the 27-year-old venture capitalist made a start-up to help: BetterBoyfriend.me. This service sends girlfriends and wives a gift every month for about $70.

Sullivan works in finance. He has been testing the program quietly. About 350 boyfriends have joined. The service makes about $17,000 each month. Most members are his Harvard friends and investor friends. Many are venture capitalists, founders, or employees at companies like Apple, Google, and Facebook. Their girlfriends have been getting gifts for the last year.

Each month, members choose one gift from a list of seven. The gifts include chocolate, tea sets, and manicures. The package is sent to the boyfriend with no brand name and no label.

Sullivan tests ideas by buying Facebook ads to see if people are interested. BetterBoyfriend.me became popular fast. Sullivan said it solved a problem many people have.

BetterBoyfriend.me was not his first idea. For a few days, he thought about a dog-sharing idea. He said he saw rich guys looking miserable walking dogs. He said a dog is only valuable 5% of the time. He thought about paying someone to babysit dogs, and letting other people rent them.

Another start-up is called InvisibleBoyfriend. It lets users create a believable social partner who is always available to talk. The result is a “boyfriend” who can text with you all day.

Founder Kyle Tabor started it to help users stop unwanted advances. But he found that people became attached to their computer-made partners. He said many users want companionship through conversation, not just proof of a relationship.

For Sullivan and Tabor, a surprise was the real relationships they formed with customers. Sullivan says he now feels like a kind of relationship consultant for the boyfriends.

Sullivan says he made mistakes. At first, the packages included receipts with his name, Dan Sullivan. One boyfriend wrote to him and said, “Listen, dude, she’s not mad but Cynthia found out.”

Sullivan says about 50% of the women who get the packages know he is involved. He says this connects with age. He thinks that after people have been married for a while, they do not keep many secrets.

Over the year, Sullivan says he has gotten to know the boyfriends very well. They even sent a gift to a hospital delivery room. He says the most important thing is that his relationship is with the boyfriend.

When he first started the company, he put tags that said BetterBoyfriend.me on flower bouquets. He went to Dolores Park in the Mission District, a popular place for tech and young people.

He looked for couples and gave the flowers to the girl. The boyfriend did not like that. Sullivan said it made sense. So he changed his plan and started giving the flowers to the boyfriends.

Over the year, Sullivan says he has gotten to know the boyfriends very well. They even delivered a package to a hospital delivery room. He said the key is to remember that his relationship is with the boyfriend.

When he first started the company, he put BetterBoyfriend.me tags on flower bouquets. Then he went to Dolores Park, a popular relaxing place in the Mission District, which has many tech workers and young people.

He said, “I looked for couples and gave the bouquet to the girl but that didn’t go down well with the boyfriend. Like, not at all. It made sense, though. It was cool. So, I reconsidered and started handing them out to the boyfriends.”

The controversial catching and killing of sharks longer than three metres began after what the state government called an ÒunprecedentedÓ number of shark attacks on Western AustraliaÕs coast, which saw a 35-year-old surfer killed in November 2013. He was the sixth person to die from a shark attack in two years.

However, according to the Shark Attack File, Australia as a whole has averaged one shark- related fatality a year for the last 50 years.Kate Faehrmann, a board member at the Sea Shepherd Conservation Society, said from a protest in Perth: ÒWeÕve been saying all along that this policy wonÕt work. Drumlines, used to catch the sharks, are indiscriminate killers. TheyÕll kill sharks whether theyÕre one, two, three metres or more, as well as dolphins, turtles and other things. ThatÕs why the community doesnÕt want it.Ó

Thousands of people protested on PerthÕs Cottesloe Beach and SydneyÕs Manly Beach,as well as hundreds at Glenelg, in south-west Adelaide, and at beaches in Victoria and Queensland. Faehrmann said the protests had shown Australians wanted sharks protected: ÒWhatÕs amazing is so many people in Australia love sharks. This has demonstrated something about the national psyche, that, despite Jaws, despite all the fear, people are coming out in their thousands across the country to say, ÔThatÕs their ocean. We respect them, we love them and we donÕt want them killed.ÕÓ Anthony Joyce, a surfer who once had his foot caught in a sharkÕs mouth, said: ÒThe number of sharks they are going to kill is going to make no difference in the schemeof things.Ó

simplified_text:
Opposition to Western Australia’s shark cull has grown. Thousands of people went to beaches around Australia to ask the state’s premier to stop the policy. RSPCA Australia and Virgin Atlantic owner Richard Branson also spoke against it.

The policy catches and kills sharks longer than three metres. It began after what the state government called an “unprecedented” number of shark attacks on Western Australia’s coast. In November 2013, a 35-year-old surfer was killed. He was the sixth person to die from a shark attack in two years.

However, the Shark Attack File says that across all of Australia, there has been about one shark-related death a year on average for the last 50 years.

Kate Faehrmann from the Sea Shepherd Conservation Society spoke at a protest in Perth. She said the policy will not work. She said drumlines, which are used to catch sharks, can kill many animals. She said they can catch and kill sharks of any size, and also dolphins, turtles, and other animals.

People protested at Perth’s Cottesloe Beach and Sydney’s Manly Beach. Hundreds also protested at Glenelg in south-west Adelaide, and at beaches in Victoria and Queensland. Faehrmann said the protests showed many Australians want sharks protected. She said that even though people fear sharks, many people are coming out to say the ocean belongs to sharks too, and they do not want sharks killed.

Anthony Joyce, a surfer who once had his foot caught in a shark’s mouth, said the number of sharks killed will not make a real difference.

The state government has refused to give a running count of how many sharks have been killed. There have been reports that sharks smaller than three metres have been released after being caught. Drumlines are floating drums anchored to the sea bed, with bait on hooks under them.

Conservationists say there is no evidence the cull will reduce shark attacks on humans, and no earlier cull has used only drumlines. Researchers at the University of Western Australia say the recent rise in shark attacks in the state may be linked to the state having the fastest-growing population in Australia, not to more sharks.

Richard Peirce, chairman of the UK charity the Shark Trust, said the cull would not work and could even bring more predators closer to the coast. He said it is sad the government did not try other ways instead of killing. He also said that leaving drumlines in the water overnight could attract other predators, drawn by injured animals on the hooks.

Around the world in 2012, there were 80 unprovoked shark attacks, and seven were fatal. This was compared to nearly 100m sharks killed by humans each year. RSPCA Australia said it believes the cull is not justified. It said there is no evidence attacks are rising because there are more sharks. Instead, it said the rise fits with changes in population and human behaviour, because more people are in the water.

Richard Branson told Fairfax Radio the policy was backfiring. He said the premier, Colin Barnett, may have thought it would help tourism, but he believed it would do the opposite. Branson said it makes the problem look bigger than it is and may stop people from visiting Perth and the surrounding countryside. He said it may only make people worry for no reason.
